questions.prompt: Why is 'default-deny + explicit allow' the recommended NetworkPolicy pattern for production clusters?

choices.text: Lateral movement containment. Without NetworkPolicies, every Pod can reach every other Pod by default — a compromised public-facing service has direct network access to databases, internal APIs, secrets endpoints, anything in the cluster. With default-deny in every namespace and explicit allow rules for each required service-to-service flow, a compromised Pod can only reach its declared dependencies on declared ports. The blast radius of any compromise drops dramatically. The cost is operational: every flow must be explicitly declared and maintained, and the initial rollout often breaks services that were silently relying on undeclared traffic. The benefit is permanent: a real security control that limits damage from breaches. For production workloads handling sensitive data, this is non-negotiable infrastructure. Combine with egress filtering to block direct internet outbound from application Pods — prevents data exfiltration even if a Pod is compromised. The discipline is heavier than open networking; the security improvement is dramatic.

questions.explanation: Default-deny + explicit allow contains lateral movement. The operational cost is real; the security benefit is permanent. Standard for production K8s clusters.

questions.prompt: A team applies default-deny NetworkPolicies and immediately half the services fail with DNS resolution errors. What's the diagnosis?

choices.text: Forgot to allow DNS in the default-deny policy. CoreDNS runs in the kube-system namespace; Pods resolve names by querying CoreDNS over UDP/TCP port 53. When you apply a default-deny egress policy, that DNS traffic is blocked along with everything else. Services can't resolve any names — including Service names within the cluster. Fix: add an allow-DNS NetworkPolicy that permits UDP/TCP port 53 egress to the kube-system namespace. Apply this everywhere you use default-deny. This is one of the most common gotchas with NetworkPolicies — the policy looks complete, services start failing in mysterious ways, and the cause is the missing DNS exception. The same goes for any cluster-wide service Pods depend on (cluster CoreDNS, observability agents, metric scrapers). Best practice: have a baseline 'allow essential cluster services' policy applied alongside default-deny in every namespace, then layer application-specific policies on top. Test the default-deny rollout in staging before production; many silently-required traffic flows surface during that exercise.

questions.explanation: Forgetting to allow DNS in deny-all setups is the classic NetworkPolicy bug. Always pair default-deny with an allow-DNS policy. Test in staging.
